posts.text:
This is family separation: ongoing, documented, and in violation of ICE's own directives. 

Read the full WRC/PHR report: womensrefugeecommission.org/…. 

Also by @maanvissingh: theguardian.com/us-news/2026…

posts.text: Workers at Honduran reception centers told researchers that if they had asked arriving deportees on any given day how many left children behind in the U.S., roughly 80 percent would raise their hands.
posts.quote: Workers at Honduran reception centers told researchers that if they had asked arriving deportees on any given day how many left children behind in the U.S., roughly 80 percent would raise their hands.

posts.text: Once deported, reunification is not guaranteed by U.S. law or policy. Honduras lacks the infrastructure, funding, and information from the U.S. government needed to systematically reunify separated families.
posts.quote: Once deported, reunification is not guaranteed by U.S. law or policy. Honduras lacks the infrastructure, funding, and information from the U.S. government needed to systematically reunify separated families.

posts.text: Pregnant women in detention reported little to no prenatal care. One woman bled for several days inside a facility before being deported with no treatment. She arrived in Honduras in emergency condition requiring immediate hospitalization.
posts.quote: Pregnant women in detention reported little to no prenatal care. One woman bled for several days inside a facility before being deported with no treatment. She arrived in Honduras in emergency condition requiring immediate hospitalization.

posts.text: All four postpartum women researchers interviewed had been separated from their infants. One mother was separated from a baby less than two months old. None were given the opportunity to bring their children with them.
posts.quote: All four postpartum women researchers interviewed had been separated from their infants. One mother was separated from a baby less than two months old. None were given the opportunity to bring their children with them.

posts.text: One mother filled out ICE Form 71-076 stating in writing that she wanted to bring her child with her. She was deported anyway, without her daughter, hands cuffed to her stomach through the night before her flight.
posts.quote: One mother filled out ICE Form 71-076 stating in writing that she wanted to bring her child with her. She was deported anyway, without her daughter, hands cuffed to her stomach through the night before her flight.

posts.text: More than half of deported parents interviewed said ICE never asked if they had children at any point during their arrest, detention, or deportation. Some were told not to speak when they tried to volunteer that information.
posts.quote: More than half of deported parents interviewed said ICE never asked if they had children at any point during their arrest, detention, or deportation. Some were told not to speak when they tried to volunteer that information.

posts.text: ICE has its own policy (the Detained Parents Directive) requiring agents to ask anyone they arrest whether they have children and to give parents the opportunity to decide what happens to those children. Researchers found routine violations of both requirements.
posts.quote: ICE has its own policy (the Detained Parents Directive) requiring agents to ask anyone they arrest whether they have children and to give parents the opportunity to decide what happens to those children. Researchers found routine violations of both requirements.

posts.text: The administration said it would target violent criminals. The data says otherwise. ICE confirms that 73% of detained individuals have no criminal convictions, and fewer than 5% have convictions for violent crimes.
posts.quote: The administration said it would target violent criminals. The data says otherwise. ICE confirms that 73% of detained individuals have no criminal convictions, and fewer than 5% have convictions for violent crimes.

posts.text: Immigration detention has reached the highest level in U.S. history. Since January 2025, the number of people detained without any criminal record has increased by approximately 2,500 percent, according to ICE's own data.
posts.quote: Immigration detention has reached the highest level in U.S. history. Since January 2025, the number of people detained without any criminal record has increased by approximately 2,500 percent, according to ICE's own data.

posts.text: A new report from the Women's Refugee Commission and Physicians for Human Rights traveled to Honduras to document what U.S. deportation policy looks like from the other side. What they found is alarming. 🧵 @P4HR
posts.quote: A new report from the Women's Refugee Commission and Physicians for Human Rights traveled to Honduras to document what U.S. deportation policy looks like from the other side. What they found is alarming. 🧵 @P4HR

posts.text: I've been tracking every ICE detention death under Trump and publishing the data publicly. Read the full breakdown, including the searchable table of all 42 deaths, at: austinkocher.substack.com/p/…
posts.quote: I've been tracking every ICE detention death under Trump and publishing the data publicly. Read the full breakdown, including the searchable table of all 42 deaths, at: austinkocher.substack.com/p/…

posts.text: Rep. Scott Franklin (FL-18) represents this facility. Call his office: (202) 225-1252. Contact Glades County Sheriff: 863-946-1600. Demand a real investigation. Don't stop until you get one.
posts.quote: Rep. Scott Franklin (FL-18) represents this facility. Call his office: (202) 225-1252. Contact Glades County Sheriff: 863-946-1600. Demand a real investigation. Don't stop until you get one.

posts.text: This is not about open borders or abolishing ICE. Congress has a legal obligation to provide oversight of ICE. Right now, that oversight is completely absent while people die in federal custody at a historically alarming rate.
posts.quote: This is not about open borders or abolishing ICE. Congress has a legal obligation to provide oversight of ICE. Right now, that oversight is completely absent while people die in federal custody at a historically alarming rate.

posts.text: A December 2025 report documented systemic abuse at Glades: physical violence, racial abuse targeting Black detainees, retaliatory punishment, and the unlawful deletion of surveillance footage. This facility never should have reopened.
posts.quote: A December 2025 report documented systemic abuse at Glades: physical violence, racial abuse targeting Black detainees, retaliatory punishment, and the unlawful deletion of surveillance footage. This facility never should have reopened.

posts.text: Glades County Detention Center was shut down by ICE in 2022 for failing to meet its own detention standards, citing persistent failures in medical care. Trump reopened it in April 2025 with no evidence those problems were fixed.
posts.quote: Glades County Detention Center was shut down by ICE in 2022 for failing to meet its own detention standards, citing persistent failures in medical care. Trump reopened it in April 2025 with no evidence those problems were fixed.

posts.text: ICE detention deaths are now occurring at a rate of roughly one every four days. Three weeks ago that rate was one every six days. The pace is accelerating, and Congress has not launched a single investigation.
posts.quote: ICE detention deaths are now occurring at a rate of roughly one every four days. Three weeks ago that rate was one every six days. The pace is accelerating, and Congress has not launched a single investigation.

posts.text:
Great work by @gregsargent.bsky.social helping to sort this out, see response from Okla. superintendent below.

BUT.. I'm not totally satisfied with this answer. The idea that ICE pulled the name of a school district in rural OK out of thin air and added it to the official 287g... dunno, vibe's off.
posts.quote:

posts.text:
There's been some alarming discussion of K-12 schools in Oklahoma entering into an agreement with ICE.

Thankfully, it's not happening. It appears ICE mistakenly posted online that it was. Oops.

Steven Cantrell, the superintendent for the Caney Valley school district in Oklahoma, emails me this:

posts.text:
I thought it was one death per week in an ICE facility.
One death every 4 days!

Will no one in Congress stop this monstrousness?

What if, once a week, ICE were to tie a prisoner to a stake in Times Square and order a firing squad to shoot?

When it's that predictable, what's the difference?

posts.text: A Federal Judge Blocked ICE's Warehouse Detention Facility Near Hagerstown, Maryland. Important update on ICE's immigrant warehouse in western Maryland: facility reconstruction has been halted under a temporary restraining order bc ICE failed to conduct an environmental study.
posts.quote: A Federal Judge Blocked ICE's Warehouse Detention Facility Near Hagerstown, Maryland. Important update on ICE's immigrant warehouse in western Maryland: facility reconstruction has been halted under a temporary restraining order bc ICE failed to conduct an environmental study.

posts.text: Mapping 41 ICE Detention Deaths During the Second Trump Administration. 41 people have died in ICE detention since January 2025. We mapped these deaths using ICE's own press releases. Here's what the data shows and why Congress needs to act.
posts.quote: Mapping 41 ICE Detention Deaths During the Second Trump Administration. 41 people have died in ICE detention since January 2025. We mapped these deaths using ICE's own press releases. Here's what the data shows and why Congress needs to act.

posts.text: A Closer Look at ICE's Enormous People Warehouse Near Hagerstown, Maryland. Watch now | I drove out to the site of one of the most controversial new ICE facilities in the country this afternoon, an 825,000 square foot warehouse built with the hopes of being used or sold to a logistics company like Amazon but which was recently purchased by Immigration and Customs and Enforcement for a million dollars to hold people instead of boxes.

posts.text: Third ICE Custody Death This Week, 11th Since January 1. Pejman Karshenas Najafabadi, a 59-year-old Iranian national, was pronounced dead on Sunday, March 1, 2026 at Merit Health Hospital in Natchez, Mississippi, becoming the eleventh person to die in ICE custody since the start of the 2026 calendar year, at a rate that now averages one detained death approximately every six days.

posts.text: Haitian Asylum Seeker Dies of Toothache in What ICE Calls "The Best Healthcare of Their Lives". Detention deaths are a foreseeable outcome of specific policy choices made by people in position of power. This post steps back to think through the broader trends and steps toward accountability.

posts.text: The Alternatives to Mass Immigrant Detention No One is Talking about w/ Claire Trickler-McNulty. Claire Trickler-McNulty and I discuss the non-detained docket, alternatives to mass detention, and why we're teaming up to research what actually works in the immigration system.

posts.text: ICE Fails Again to Publish Required Detention Data. For the second time this year, the agency has failed to publish the required information about immigration enforcement on schedule this week. Transparency isn't what you say, it's what you do.
posts.quote: ICE Fails Again to Publish Required Detention Data. For the second time this year, the agency has failed to publish the required information about immigration enforcement on schedule this week. Transparency isn't what you say, it's what you do.

posts.text: The Soft Suspension of Habeas Corpus. ICE is ignoring habeas corpus orders so routinely that a federal judge is threatening criminal contempt. New filings from Minnesota show what a de facto suspension of due process looks like.
posts.quote: The Soft Suspension of Habeas Corpus. ICE is ignoring habeas corpus orders so routinely that a federal judge is threatening criminal contempt. New filings from Minnesota show what a de facto suspension of due process looks like.

posts.text: Digging Into ICE's Messy 287(g) Data w/ Andrew Thrasher (by Andrew Thrasher). I sat down with Andrew Thrasher to talk about why ICE's 287(g) data is so hard to work with, what he's built to make it usable, and why getting this right matters for accountability.

posts.quote: ICE Warehouse in Hagerstown, MD. Watch now | Today I’m on the ground in Hagerstown, MD with The Real News Network, examining ICE’s detention reengineering program and what a planned warehouse detention facility looks like up close.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

immigration.quote:
This is family separation: ongoing, documented, and in violation of ICE's own directives. 

Read the full WRC/PHR report: womensrefugeecommission.org/…. 

Also by @maanvissingh: theguardian.com/us-news/2026…

Workers at Honduran reception centers told researchers that if they had asked arriving deportees on any given day how many left children behind in the U.S., roughly 80 percent would raise their hands.

Once deported, reunification is not guaranteed by U.S. law or policy. Honduras lacks the infrastructure, funding, and information from the U.S. government needed to systematically reunify separated families.

Pregnant women in detention reported little to no prenatal care. One woman bled for several days inside a facility before being deported with no treatment. She arrived in Honduras in emergency condition requiring immediate hospitalization.

All four postpartum women researchers interviewed had been separated from their infants. One mother was separated from a baby less than two months old. None were given the opportunity to bring their children with them.

One mother filled out ICE Form 71-076 stating in writing that she wanted to bring her child with her. She was deported anyway, without her daughter, hands cuffed to her stomach through the night before her flight.

More than half of deported parents interviewed said ICE never asked if they had children at any point during their arrest, detention, or deportation. Some were told not to speak when they tried to volunteer that information.

ICE has its own policy (the Detained Parents Directive) requiring agents to ask anyone they arrest whether they have children and to give parents the opportunity to decide what happens to those children. Researchers found routine violations of both requirements.

The administration said it would target violent criminals. The data says otherwise. ICE confirms that 73% of detained individuals have no criminal convictions, and fewer than 5% have convictions for violent crimes.

Immigration detention has reached the highest level in U.S. history. Since January 2025, the number of people detained without any criminal record has increased by approximately 2,500 percent, according to ICE's own data.

A new report from the Women's Refugee Commission and Physicians for Human Rights traveled to Honduras to document what U.S. deportation policy looks like from the other side. What they found is alarming. 🧵 @P4HR
I've been tracking every ICE detention death under Trump and publishing the data publicly. Read the full breakdown, including the searchable table of all 42 deaths, at: austinkocher.substack.com/p/…
Rep. Scott Franklin (FL-18) represents this facility. Call his office: (202) 225-1252. Contact Glades County Sheriff: 863-946-1600. Demand a real investigation. Don't stop until you get one.

This is not about open borders or abolishing ICE. Congress has a legal obligation to provide oversight of ICE. Right now, that oversight is completely absent while people die in federal custody at a historically alarming rate.

A December 2025 report documented systemic abuse at Glades: physical violence, racial abuse targeting Black detainees, retaliatory punishment, and the unlawful deletion of surveillance footage. This facility never should have reopened.

Glades County Detention Center was shut down by ICE in 2022 for failing to meet its own detention standards, citing persistent failures in medical care. Trump reopened it in April 2025 with no evidence those problems were fixed.

ICE detention deaths are now occurring at a rate of roughly one every four days. Three weeks ago that rate was one every six days. The pace is accelerating, and Congress has not launched a single investigation.

This is family separation: ongoing, documented, and in violation of ICE's own directives. 

Read the full WRC/PHR report: womensrefugeecommission.org/research-resources/what-about-my-children. 

Also by @maanvissingh: www.theguardian.com/us-news/2026...

Workers at Honduran reception centers told researchers that if they had asked arriving deportees on any given day how many left children behind in the U.S., roughly 80 percent would raise their hands.

Once deported, reunification is not guaranteed by U.S. law or policy. Honduras lacks the infrastructure, funding, and information from the U.S. government needed to systematically reunify separated families.

Pregnant women in detention reported little to no prenatal care. One woman bled for several days inside a facility before being deported with no treatment. She arrived in Honduras in emergency condition requiring immediate hospitalization.

All four postpartum women researchers interviewed had been separated from their infants. One mother was separated from a baby less than two months old. None were given the opportunity to bring their children with them.

One mother filled out ICE Form 71-076 stating in writing that she wanted to bring her child with her. She was deported anyway, without her daughter, hands cuffed to her stomach through the night before her flight.

More than half of deported parents interviewed said ICE never asked if they had children at any point during their arrest, detention, or deportation. Some were told not to speak when they tried to volunteer that information.

ICE has its own policy (the Detained Parents Directive) requiring agents to ask anyone they arrest whether they have children and to give parents the opportunity to decide what happens to those children. Researchers found routine violations of both requirements.

The administration said it would target violent criminals. The data says otherwise. ICE confirms that 73% of detained individuals have no criminal convictions, and fewer than 5% have convictions for violent crimes.

Immigration detention has reached the highest level in U.S. history. Since January 2025, the number of people detained without any criminal record has increased by approximately 2,500 percent, according to ICE's own data.

A new report from the Women's Refugee Commission and Physicians for Human Rights traveled to Honduras to document what U.S. deportation policy looks like from the other side. What they found is alarming. 🧵
Yep. There are definitely remaining unanswered questions here about ICE's handling of it and all of it, really

Great work by @gregsargent.bsky.social helping to sort this out, see response from Okla. superintendent below.

BUT.. I'm not totally satisfied with this answer. The idea that ICE pulled the name of a school district in rural OK out of thin air and added it to the official 287g... dunno, vibe's off.

There's been some alarming discussion of K-12 schools in Oklahoma entering into an agreement with ICE.

Thankfully, it's not happening. It appears ICE mistakenly posted online that it was. Oops.

Steven Cantrell, the superintendent for the Caney Valley school district in Oklahoma, emails me this:

I thought it was one death per week in an ICE facility.
One death every 4 days!

Will no one in Congress stop this monstrousness?

What if, once a week, ICE were to tie a prisoner to a stake in Times Square and order a firing squad to shoot?

When it's that predictable, what's the difference?
